perturbation.original_text: 1.6.1. Calculation. The amount of the Monthly Compensation shall be determined by taking average gross profit related to the relevant Products for each of the monthly periods completed since the beginning of this Agreement, where "gross profit" shall mean the difference between the revenue generated by Distributor during the relevant period less any commissions paid by Distributor to third parties and less the Price paid by Distributor to Vendor for the relevant Products (exclusive of any taxes, charges, fees or impositions related to sales or delivery).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6.1. Calculation. The amount of the Monthly Compensation will be negotiated in good faith between Vendor and Distributor.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the definition of 'gross profit' and the specific calculation method for Monthly Compensation, the modified text introduces ambiguity and relies on future negotiation, contradicting the original intent of a fixed compensation formula. This creates uncertainty because it is unclear how gross profit will be determined now, if at all, potentially leading to disputes. This omission directly affects Section 1.6, which promises Monthly Compensation. Without a calculation method, the promise becomes vague and unenforceable as to amount. This weakens the protection for the Distributor upon termination under sections 1.1.4 or 1.1.5, where without the definition of the calculation of this compensation, the Vendor can argue the definition of compensation, and therefore breaching the section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.6. Monthly Compensation. In the event that this Agreement is terminated pursuant to either Section 1.1.4 or 1.1.5 of this Agreement, this shall not of itself be deemed a breach hereof but Distributor shall have the right to receive monthly compensation from Vendor, or successor to the Vendor, representing the amount of profit to Distributor lost as a result of the termination (the "Monthly Compensation").
perturbation.justification: The original text defined how "Monthly Compensation" was calculated. The changed text removes this calculation and replaces it with a negotiation between Vendor and Distributor. Section 1.6 states that the Distributor "shall have the right to receive monthly compensation... representing the amount of profit to Distributor lost as a result of the termination." By removing the calculation in 1.6.1, the amount of profit is now ambiguous, contradicting the fixed calculation that would allow the distributor to have the 'right to receive' it.

perturbation.original_text:
8.4. Defective Products. Distributor may, no later than one hundred and twenty (120) calendar days after a Product is sold by Distributor to a customer, return to Vendor, at Distributor's expense, any Product received by Distributor from Vendor during the 120-day period prior to such return, which Distributor or its customer believes to be defective. In the event that such Product is defective, i.e., the failure of a Product to operate in accordance with its published specifications, Vendor shall:
8.4.1. Reimburse Distributor for the costs of freight reasonably incurred by Distributor in returning the Product to Vendor; and
8.4.2. Issue Distributor a credit against future purchases in an amount equal to the purchase price paid by Distributor for the Product or, as requested by Distributor, full reimbursement for the defective Products.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.4. Defective Products. Distributor may return to Vendor, at Distributor's expense, any Product which Distributor or its customer believes to be defective. In the event that such Product is defective, Vendor shall determine the appropriate course of action, which may include repair, replacement, or credit. Vendor's decision will be final.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the 120-day return window and the specific obligations of the Vendor (reimbursement of freight and credit/reimbursement of purchase price). It introduces Vendor's sole discretion in determining the 'appropriate course of action'. This contradicts Section 10.3, where Vendor warrants Products are fit for purpose and that Distributor, in cooperation with Vendor, will remedy any defect. By making the Vendor's decision final, it can avoid those requirements, and making section 10.3 warranty irrelevant, since Vendor can decide not to remedy the defect.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10.3. Product Warranty. Vendor warrants to Distributor that the Products shall perform without manufacturing failure and are fit for purpose. Distributor, in cooperation with Vendor will remedy any defect in the Product in accordance with Section 8.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 8.4 removes the defined 120-day return window and Vendor's obligation to reimburse freight and issue credit, replacing it with Vendor's sole discretion in determining the 'appropriate course of action'. This directly contradicts Section 10.3, which states that 'Distributor, in cooperation with Vendor will remedy any defect in the Product in accordance with Section 8.' By granting Vendor the final decision and removing the defined remedies, Section 8.4 allows Vendor to potentially avoid remedying defects as outlined in Section 10.3, creating a strong conflict and rendering the warranty in Section 10.3 potentially unenforceable or irrelevant.

perturbation.changed_text: 5.1. Purchase. In exchange for the exclusive distribution right and license granted to Distributor pursuant to this Agreement, during the Term, Distributor hereby agrees to purchase the Products from Vendor as may be submitted by Distributor to Vendor pursuant to a Purchase Order (as defined herein).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific purchase commitments outlined in Sections 5.1.1 through 5.1.4, the agreement removes the minimum order requirements. This contradicts Section 5.5, which outlines the consequences of the Distributor failing to meet its purchase obligations specified in Section 5.1, where Vendor can convert the agreement from exclusive to non-exclusive. Because now, there is no amount of purchases, Vendor cannot take on the action to turn this agreement to non-exclusive.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.5. Failure to Purchase. If Distributor fails to purchase or complete the purchase of the Products (or the payment thereof) in the quantities and the times specified in Section 5.1 of this Agreement, Vendor shall have the right to convert this Agreement from an exclusive grant of distribution rights to a non-exclusive grant of distribution rights by providing written notice to Distributor within fourteen (14) days of any date by which Distributor was obliged to purchase the specified quantity of Products as set in Section 5.1 of this Agreement. Upon providing notice to Distributor pursuant to this Section 5.5, Distributor shall no longer be required to purchase balance of the Products required to be purchased pursuant to Section 5.1 of this Agreement. Distributor shall not be liable for any losses or damages incurred by Vendor as a result of Distributor's failure to meet its obligations under Section 5.1 of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the specific purchase commitments outlined in the original Section 5.1. This directly contradicts Section 5.5, which provides Vendor the right to convert the agreement from exclusive to non-exclusive if Distributor fails to meet the purchase obligations specified in Section 5.1. Because the changed text removes the purchase commitment, the vendor cannot turn the agreement to non-exclusive.
